learning_outcomes.description: Describe key features of theoretical and conceptual frameworks for understanding categories of diversity (e.g., race, gender), and the intersections of these
Source, review, and critically evaluate/apply research addressing the implications of marginalisation, inequality, and colonisation on criminal justice outcomes
Demonstrate enhanced skills of problem solving, critical thinking, and reflection in diverse contexts
Demonstrate strong communication skills across diverse modes and settings

assessments.due_date:
In-class Reflection Activity # 1 (Week 2) 6/08/2026 2:00 pm

In-class Reflection Activity # 2 (Week 4) 20/08/2026 2:00 pm

In-class Reflection Activity # 3 (Week 6) 3/09/2026 2:00 pm

Assignments will be submitted by the end of the seminars in weeks 2, 4, and 6. Students will be given time to complete the assessment in-class at the end of these seminars. Students will submit by providing their written work to the teaching staff at the end of each of these three seminars.
assessments.task_description:
Assignment 1 (consisting of 3 x in-class short-answer reflection activities) is worth 45% of your grade overall: 15% per activity (45% overall, combined).

Each short-answer reflection will be conducted / responded to by students in the seminar; students must attend the seminar to be able to complete the activity in the weeks that they are due. Students who have a reasonable excuse for being unable to attend a seminar to complete the assignment should reach out to the Course Coordinator for an alternative arrangement. Students with SAPs in place may be eligible for alternative arrangements; please contact the Course Coordinator ASAP.

The short-answer reflection activities may be based on learning materials from the week they are due, and/or from any of the weeks prior. Students should attend all seminars to ensure are best placed to complete Assignment 1.

Word count: Each reflection response will be 300 words in length (+/-10%) for all students (900 words total across 3 x papers).

Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This assessment task will be completed in-person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The completed in-class reflection activities must be submitted by the end of the seminars in the weeks that they are due. Students will be given time towards the end of these seminars to complete and submit the assignments. (Please note, students with SAPs and/or approved extensions may be given alternative arrangements; please contact the Course Coordinator ASAP. Students who have a reasonable excuse for being unable to join a seminar in the weeks that an Assignment 1 reflection activity is due should email the Course Coordinator ASAP to request alternative arrangements.)
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Participation/ Student contribution
assessments.task_description:
Students will be asked to move from analysis to future thinking-oriented action in weeks 9, 10 and 11. In weeks 10 and 11 in particular, students will arrange into smaller groups to design 'fairer justice futures'. The focus topics for these weeks include: i) crime prevention, and ii) policing. Students will be required to imagine and design 'fairer justice futures' proposals in relation to each of these two topics across the two weeks of seminars (weeks 10, 11). Students will be encouraged to use their creativity and apply the concepts of 'fugitive' and 'utopic' thinking - covered in early weeks of the semester - when developing their proposals.

To be eligible for the 10% pass/fail (non-graded) mark, students must do the following in each of the two weeks (10, 11):

  • attend the seminar,
  • during the allocated time in the seminar, work in a smaller group to develop a short proposal -- using the template provided on Blackboard -- that in some way addresses the social inequalities discussed in previous weeks to achieve a fairer justice future,
  • as a group, upload their proposal to the provided class Padlet page by the end of the relevant seminar, and
  • in each of the weeks (and by the end of each respective week), engage with at least two other groups' proposals displayed on the class Padlet by undertaking a peer-review - adding constructive comments/feedback on the class Padlet page.


To complete this assignment and receive their mark, each student is required to individually upload evidence of their Padlet posts and comments/engagement for the two weeks (10, 11) in week 12, using the template provided on Blackboard. This evidence is required to be uploaded via the provided Turnitin link in the Assignment 2 folder on Blackboard.

Students will not be graded on this assignment; if they individually submit evidence of having completed the above tasks, they will receive the 10% available for this assignment. If they do not successfully submit evidence of their completion of the above tasks in each of the two weeks (10,11), they will receive zero marks for this assignment.

Students who have a reasonable excuse for being unable to attend one of the seminars in weeks 10 or 11 (when Assignment 2 activities will be completed) should let the Course Coordinator know ASAP by email, and seek an alternative arrangement. Students with SAPs may also require adjustments to this task; please also contact the Course Coordinator if this is you.

AI Use: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which AI may provide limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy. Students may be required to complete an AI usage declaration and submit it with their assessment. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Students must engage in the Assignment 2 activities in the seminars from weeks 9-11.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Individually, students will work with the ideas they gathered during Assignment 2 (group work) -- as well as feedback on Assignment 2 received from their peers -- to develop a fuller individual researched proposal for a fairer justice future. Their proposal must relate, broadly, to one of the topics covered in weeks 9-11: i) crime prevention, ii) policing, or ii) abolitionist/alternative futures. A proposal template for this final assignment will be provided via Blackboard. Students MUST use this template for their proposal submission.

This assignment will be graded according to the rubric provided on Blackboard.

Word count: Individual proposals are 2,000 words in length (+/- 10%) for CRIM2099 students; 2500 words in length (+/- 10%) for CRIM7099 students.

The week 12 seminar will be dedicated to scaffolding students' successful completion of this final assignment. Students will then have weeks 13 and 14 to continue work on the assignment, before it is due at the beginning of week 15.

AI Use: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which AI may provide limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy. Students may be required to complete an AI usage declaration and submit it with their assessment. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This assessment item must be submitted via Turnitin on Blackboard Ultra.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.
